structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (Jan 2019–Dec 2023). All patient admissions with complete demographic, treatment, and 30-day readmission status labels. Exclude records with >20% missing values in key features or incomplete discharge summaries.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge (yes/no). Defined as any unplanned hospital admission occurring ≤30 days after the index discharge date, identified via admission records. Planned readmissions (documented as scheduled procedures) are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by department. Rationale: (1) Temporal split prevents label leakage—a patient's future readmission status cannot inform the training set. (2) Stratification by department ensures each split retains the confound (department variability) at consistent proportions, allowing the model to learn department-specific patterns rather than being biased by imbalanced department representation. Split: 60% train (Jan 2019–Dec 2021, n≈30,000), 20% validation (Jan 2022–Aug 2022, n≈10,000), 20% test (Sep 2022–Dec 2023, n≈10,000). Within each split, stratify to preserve the original department distribution and readmission rate.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaler, categorical encoder, imputer) are fit on the training set only. The fitted transformers are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage of test-set statistics into the training process.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: patient age, gender, admission type, primary diagnosis (ICD-10 code, one-hot encoded), secondary diagnoses (count and top 5 codes), length of stay, number of medications prescribed, comorbidity count (Charlson index), insurance type, department. Engineered features: (1) length-of-stay category (binned: <2, 2–7, 7–14, >14 days), (2) recent admission history (number of admissions in prior 12 months), (3) medication complexity score (count × polypharmacy flag), (4) department-level readmission rate (population mean, fit on train set only to avoid leakage). All numeric features are standardized (z-score) using train-set statistics. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing values in numeric features are imputed with train-set median; missing categorical values are imputed with a 'missing' category.
structured_choices.model: Hybrid CNN-RNN architecture: (1) CNN component processes patient treatment histories (sequences of procedures/medication administration over admission window) as learned feature extractors. (2) RNN (LSTM) component captures temporal dependencies in the sequential treatment data. (3) Dense layers combine CNN/RNN outputs with static demographic features. Output layer: single sigmoid neuron for binary readmission prediction. Hyperparameters: 2 CNN blocks (filters: 32, 64; kernel size 3), 1 LSTM layer (units: 64), 2 dense layers (128, 32 units with ReLU), dropout (0.3), learning rate 0.001, batch size 32.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same feature set as the hybrid model (demographics + engineered features, excluding sequence data because logistic regression cannot natively process sequences). The logistic regression baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 regularization strength (C) is tuned via grid search on the validation set over C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}. Both models are trained on identical train/val/test splits. This ensures a fair comparison: the baseline is not handicapped by poor tuning or feature engineering, and the hybrid model's advantage (if any) reflects its architectural capacity, not resource disparity.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Rationale: (1) Readmission data are imbalanced (assume ~15–20% readmission rate); AUPRC is insensitive to class imbalance, whereas accuracy or raw AUC-ROC can be misleading. (2) From a clinical standpoint, hospital administrators care about the trade-off between precision (avoiding false alarms that waste intervention resources) and recall (catching true high-risk patients). AUPRC directly quantifies this trade-off. (3) AUPRC does not require threshold selection, making it threshold-agnostic for model comparison.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at fixed specificity (e.g., 90% specificity) to reflect the clinical constraint that false positive interventions are costly
F1-score (macro) to balance precision and recall, weighted toward minority class
Calibration: Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots to assess whether predicted probabilities reflect true readmission probabilities (important for clinical decision-making)
Feature attribution via SHAP values for the hybrid model and permutation importance for logistic regression (to address interpretability requirement)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both models is performed on the validation set. Grid search or random search is conducted over the validation set only; test set is never touched during tuning. The model checkpoint with the best AUPRC on the validation set is selected and evaluated once on the held-out test set. Cross-validation is not used here because temporal structure would be violated by random k-fold splits; temporal split is the appropriate strategy.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and not examined until final evaluation. A single evaluation run is performed: both the tuned hybrid model and tuned logistic regression baseline are applied to the test set, and metrics are computed once. No further tuning, threshold adjustment, or model selection occurs after test-set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Department variability is a known confound. This design addresses it via stratified splitting (department proportions preserved in each split) and feature inclusion (department is included as a one-hot encoded feature). Additionally, department-level readmission rates (population means computed from training data) are engineered as a feature, allowing both models to account for department-specific baseline risk. Sensitivity analysis: performance is evaluated separately for each department on the test set to verify that the hybrid model does not spuriously improve performance in some departments while degrading in others. If one department's sample size is very small (<500 records), it is flagged as a limitation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Hybrid CNN-RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use electronic health records (EHR) from a single hospital system spanning 5 calendar years (January 2019 through December 2023). The dataset comprises approximately 50,000 patient admission records, each labeled with 30-day readmission status (yes/no). Readmission is defined operationally as any unplanned hospital admission occurring within 30 days of the index discharge date. Planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled procedures) are excluded to avoid mislabeling.

Data quality issues are handled as follows: records with >20% missing values in key demographic or clinical fields are excluded. Missing values in numeric features are imputed using the training-set median; missing categorical values receive an explicit 'missing' category. This approach avoids test-set leakage of imputation statistics.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split temporally to prevent label leakage: training (60%, Jan 2019–Dec 2021; n ≈ 30,000), validation (20%, Jan 2022–Aug 2022; n ≈ 10,000), and test (20%, Sep 2022–Dec 2023; n ≈ 10,000). Temporal splitting reflects the deployment scenario—the model is trained on historical data and applied to future admissions.

Within each temporal window, we stratify by hospital department to ensure that department-level variability (a known confound) is represented proportionally in train, validation, and test sets. This prevents one split from being composed primarily of low-complexity departments while another concentrates high-complexity cases, which would bias performance estimates.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features combine static demographics (age, gender, insurance type) and clinical signals. Static features include admission type, primary and secondary diagnoses (ICD-10 codes, one-hot encoded), length of stay, medication count, comorbidity burden (Charlson index), and department assignment.

Engineered features are constructed to capture domain-relevant patterns: (1) length-of-stay category (bins: <2 days, 2–7, 7–14, >14 days) to capture admission intensity; (2) recent admission history (count of admissions in the prior 12 months) to proxy chronic disease burden; (3) medication complexity score combining polypharmacy count and flags; (4) department-level readmission baseline (population mean computed from training data only), allowing both models to adjust for department-specific risk.

All numeric features are standardized (z-score) using training-set statistics. All preprocessing transformers—scaler, encoder, imputer—are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This is critical for avoiding data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hybrid model is a CNN-RNN architecture: a convolutional neural network processes temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications) as feature extractors, feeding into an LSTM recurrent layer to capture temporal dependencies. Dense layers integrate CNN/RNN outputs with static features, culminating in a sigmoid output for binary prediction. Architecture: 2 CNN blocks (32 and 64 filters, kernel size 3), 1 LSTM layer (64 units), 2 dense layers (128 and 32 units with ReLU), dropout (0.3), learning rate 0.001.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on identical features (excluding raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot process natively, but including all engineered features). Logistic regression is tuned via grid search over L2 regularization strength (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) on the validation set. This ensures the baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter optimization effort and fair feature engineering, making any performance gap attributable to the hybrid model's architecture rather than tuning disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Readmission data are imbalanced (approximately 15–20% positive rate). AUPRC is insensitive to class imbalance and directly reflects the clinically relevant trade-off between precision (avoiding false alarms that waste intervention resources) and recall (identifying true high-risk patients). This aligns with hospital administrators' operational goal: resource-constrained intervention programs need both sensitivity and specificity.

Secondary metrics include: (1) sensitivity at 90% specificity, reflecting the clinical constraint that false positives are costly; (2) F1-score (macro-averaged) for balanced precision-recall assessment; (3) calibration diagnostics (Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true readmission risk—essential for clinicians interpreting model outputs; (4) feature attribution via SHAP values (hybrid model) and permutation importance (logistic regression) to address interpretability requirements.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning for both models occurs on the validation set only. The model configuration achieving the best AUPRC on the validation set is selected. The test set is held out and accessed only once, after tuning is complete. No cross-validation is performed on the full dataset because temporal structure would be violated by random k-fold splits; temporal split is methodologically appropriate here.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Department variability is addressed via stratified splitting and feature inclusion. Department-level baseline readmission rates (computed from training data) are incorporated as features, enabling both models to learn department-specific risk adjustments. Sensitivity analysis evaluates performance within each department on the test set to verify the hybrid model does not improve performance in some departments while degrading in others. Small departments (n < 500) are flagged as analytical limitations.

The experiment establishes whether the hybrid model achieves higher AUPRC than logistic regression on held-out test data. It does not establish whether the hybrid model is clinically deployable without human validation, whether the performance gains persist across hospitals with different EHR systems, or whether the model remains fair across demographic subgroups—those are separate validation steps.
